author: tersec
content:
One general concern about the order-dependency: some are not order-dependent in any intrinsic way, but throw off either REJECT or IGNORE detectably. Nimbus intentionally rearranges these sometimes because various gossip validations have performancewise suboptimal ordering for efficiently rejecting messages. But this can change whether one sees REJECT or IGNORE, even though the net effect on the network is comparable (not identical).

The right answer is ultimately to address these in the spec, but in the meantime, we're not going to randomly pessimize our code

author: tersec
content: But also, the "address these in the spec" strarts venturing towards a kind of performance optimization it's not clear the spec should even have

author: terencechain
content:
this is a good spec test candidate, we found out about this bug today. For attestations in block, we missed the following check. 
```
if block_slot == attestation.data.slot:
        assert attestation.data.index == 0
```
that means a bad proposer/attester could propose a block, and pack an attestation such that its block slot == attestation slot, but yet vote for 1, and in our fork choice accounting, we would have chosen full over empty even majority PTC voted for not available 
<@592004585506340885>

author: nero_eth
content:
Regarding the builder deposit contract discussed today: this is a different discussion than the "what to do with CLs not being able to process X deposits coming from the EL" one. Am I right on that? 
Like, independently from what we do with builders, if we change nothing in the 8192 deposits from validators, the problem persists.

author: nflaig
content: you can still have >8192 validator deposits per payload so that limit still needs to be dealt with (if we want >194M gas limit) but this resolves issues due to expensive signature verification, see https://discord.com/channels/595666850260713488/1514209319283982516

author: nero_eth
content: ok, so it is fair to 2 those two topics are orthogonal?

author: nflaig
content: I mean if we go with EIP-8282, the remaining issue to solve is just the 8192 sszlimit itself, so we can either bump that constant or go with EIP-7688

author: nero_eth
content: or we hard-cap it on the EL and make blocks invalid that include more than we're fine with

author: nflaig
content: I thought that idea was rejected to have such a cap as it makes block building harder for ELs?

author: nero_eth
content:
It depends where we set that cap.
E.g. with eip-7825, the tx gas limit is 2**24. Assuming a deposit costs 23k gas, one can do ~730 deposits per tx.
If we put the deposit cap at something above that, e.g. 1028, there's no DoS as for each tx, a builder could take the block gas limit / 23k and assume the tx goes all-in on deposits, while at the same time keeping track of the nr of deposits. If gaslimit / 23k > available deposits, one doesn't simulate the tx. 
This closes the loophole and since there are never such deposit-heavy blocks in practice, everything is fine.

author: nero_eth
content: The worst-case deposits are inferable from the tx.gasLimit and the deposit cost

author: potuz
content:
I think the discussion around the economic viability of the builder deposit attack is very much misguided. The block price has been 0.01 ETH forever, and filling it with garbage transactions is also quite cheap.  At these prices, an attacker willing to burn 50KETH will buy every single block for the next 19 years. 

a) Why do we know this will not happen? simply because of economic pressure: users want their transactions included, they pay more, builders make money bidding higher and therefore honest builder start bidding and thus the attacker's cost is higher. Do we need to trust the builders to bid higher? no we don't, we trust that at least some users send high paying transactions to the mempool or that at least one buillder bids higher. 

b) What happens when the builder gets an honest block on chain? well, the attacker has spent some of his money preventing liveness up to that point, but gets to keep the rest or decide if they will continue the attack. 

The situation in the deposit flow attack is even worse for the attacker. They need to upfront their 50K ETH. Now, as blocks stop landing, the price for inclusion continues to rise up, now any builder/local proposer that decides to verify signatures before including transactions can land a block and make all of the money,  The longer the liveness issue the faster the race to include an executable block since the larger the price. 

a') Do we need to rely on builders being honest? no, we, just as above, rely on them being rational. 

b') What happens when the chain recovers liveness? the attacker has lost all of the 50K ETH

author: potuz
content: I honestly feel that we are going over this issue just because it's an ugly thing to have in the spec, we should have gone with a separate contract since the start, but now we have this and really doing malabarisms just over some theoretical attack that is irrational does not make sense to me.

author: wemeetagain
content: The problem is that the attack is real and we need real counters to the attacks in our codebases to deter them, and we need to maintain that code, and we need to actually test that code. All of this takes time. Time that we could instead use doing the right thing, which is avoiding having an attackable spec in the first place. The change really is quite small, it just touches several layers: deploying a system contract, calling the contract at the end of block, packing that info into execution requests, passing thru to the CL. But all of those things are wiring.  Wiring that has already been done before in EIP 7251. The tradeoff is obvious here. Just deploy the contract and add wiring. Don't force new caches and introduce new testing and attack surface.

author: potuz
content: I disagree with that being the trade-off, the trade-off is coordination and delay. If everyone is clear on this then I don't mind further delaying this fork

author: jtraglia
content: In my opinion, EIP-8282 is a good solution. It's what we should have done originally. It will allow us to further scale the gas limit without repeatedly running benchmarks & improving complex optimizations. I think we should bite the bullet & make this change quickly.

author: jtraglia
content:
Here's a draft PR on which we can review/discuss things:
https://github.com/ethereum/consensus-specs/pull/5359

content: aren't you forcing a cache anyway at the fork boundary?

author: potuz
content: The deposit contract has logic to send logs pre genesis, we can probably do the same with the new contract and avoid the fork attack also.

author: pk910
content:
Yea, I agree with this.
To be fair, the single deposit contract would have worked nicely without the signature validation issue.
But given the uncovered problem, EIP-8282 is the cleaner approach.

It is an expensive attack,  but still possible and effective (with many invalid sigs so lost funds).
The question is if we want to accept delaying the fork further or not.
Adding a new contract with pending audit and review/improvement loops will take some time..
